title: Ray Wylie Hubbard

Musical artist

**Ray Wylie Hubbard** (born November 13, 1946) is an American singer and songwriter.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ray_Wylie_Hubbard&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Hubbard was born on November 13, 1946, in [Soper, Oklahoma](/wiki/Soper,_Oklahoma "Soper, Oklahoma").[[2]](#cite_note-allmusic-2) His family moved to [Oak Cliff](/wiki/Oak_Cliff "Oak Cliff") in southwest [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas") in 1954. He attended [W. H. Adamson High School](/wiki/W._H._Adamson_High_School "W. H. Adamson High School") with [Michael Martin Murphey](/wiki/Michael_Martin_Murphey "Michael Martin Murphey"), [Larry Groce](/wiki/Larry_Groce "Larry Groce"), [B. W. Stevenson](/wiki/B._W._Stevenson "B. W. Stevenson"), and [Owen Temple](/wiki/Owen_Temple "Owen Temple").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Hubbard graduated in 1965 and enrolled in North Texas State University (now the [University of North Texas](/wiki/University_of_North_Texas "University of North Texas")) in [Denton](/wiki/Denton,_Texas "Denton, Texas") as an [English](/wiki/English_studies "English studies") major. He spent the summers in [Red River, New Mexico](/wiki/Red_River,_New_Mexico "Red River, New Mexico"), playing folk music in hootenannies with a trio known as Three Faces West.[[4]](#cite_note-tucker-4)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ray_Wylie_Hubbard&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ray_Wylie_Hubbard&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1970s")]

While he was in New Mexico, Hubbard wrote "Up Against the Wall, Redneck Mother"[[4]](#cite_note-tucker-4) first made famous by [Jerry Jeff Walker](/wiki/Jerry_Jeff_Walker "Jerry Jeff Walker")'s 1973 recording, and covered by a wide variety of other artists since.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Bolstered by the success of the song, he was signed by [Warner Bros. Records](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Records "Warner Bros. Records"). Hubbard then assembled a band of friends and locals and, in 1976, released *Ray Wylie Hubbard and the Cowboy Twinkies.*[[6]](#cite_note-Christgau-6) Unbeknownst to Hubbard, producer Michael Brovsky had decided to "Nashville-ize" the sound by adding overdub mixes and female backup singers to the recordings. The result was "a botched sound" that Hubbard disapproved of vehemently, but the album was released despite his attempts to block it.[[4]](#cite_note-tucker-4)

### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ray_Wylie_Hubbard&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1980s")]

Hubbard then recorded albums for various other labels for the next decade, but struggled with the sales of his mix of [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"), [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") and [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues").[[7]](#cite_note-Jurek-7) The last album he recorded in the 1980s was *Caught in the Act* (1984) on his newly formed *Misery Loves Company* record label.

### 1990s and beyond

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ray_Wylie_Hubbard&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1990s and beyond")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Ray_Wylie_Hubbard.jpg/250px-Ray_Wylie_Hubbard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ray_Wylie_Hubbard.jpg)

Hubbard performing at the Cactus Cafe in Austin, August 2009

He returned to recording in the early 1990s, and released his album *Lost Train of Thought* in 1992,[[4]](#cite_note-tucker-4) followed by *Loco Gringo's Lament* in 1994. Eventually a steady following began to re-discover Hubbard's music and he has been recording steadily since.

He describes his 2017 album *Tell the Devil I'm Getting There as Fast as I Can* as rock & roll, though his style has become associated with [outlaw country](/wiki/Outlaw_country "Outlaw country").[[8]](#cite_note-Gage-8)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ray_Wylie_Hubbard&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Hubbard was first married to D'Ann Griffin, with whom he had a son, Cory. Hubbard and Judy Stone married on February 18, 1989. They have a son, Lucas, who plays lead guitar in Hubbard's band. As of 2024, the couple maintains homes in [Taos, New Mexico](/wiki/Taos,_New_Mexico "Taos, New Mexico") and [Wimberley, Texas](/wiki/Wimberley,_Texas "Wimberley, Texas").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

He struggled with [alcoholism](/wiki/Alcoholism "Alcoholism") and cocaine addiction throughout his thirties as his first marriage collapsed and both of his parents died. Ultimately he decided on his 41st birthday (in 1987) to get sober, crediting fellow musician [Stevie Ray Vaughan](/wiki/Stevie_Ray_Vaughan "Stevie Ray Vaughan") (who sobered up approximately a year earlier) for taking him to his first [Alcoholics Anonymous](/wiki/Alcoholics_Anonymous "Alcoholics Anonymous") meeting.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Discography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ray_Wylie_Hubbard&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Discography")]

* 1975 *Ray Wylie Hubbard and the Cowboy Twinkies* – [Warner Bros. Records](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Records "Warner Bros. Records")[[6]](#cite_note-Christgau-6)
* 1978 *Off the Wall* – Lone Star Records/[Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_Records "Mercury Records")/[PolyGram](/wiki/PolyGram "PolyGram")[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* 1979 *Something About the Night* – Renegade Records
* 1984 *Caught in the Act* – Misery Loves Company Records
* 1992 *Lost Train of Thought* – Misery Loves Company/DejaDisc Records
* 1994 *Loco Gringo's Lament* – Misery Loves Company/DejaDisc Records
* 1997 *Dangerous Spirits* – [Rounder](/wiki/Rounder_Records "Rounder Records")/[Philo Records](/wiki/Philo_Records_(folk) "Philo Records (folk)")[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* 1998 *Live at Cibolo Creek* – Misery Loves Company
* 1999 *Crusades of the Restless Knights* – Rounder/Philo Records
* 2001 *Eternal and Lowdown* – Rounder/Philo Records[[13]](#cite_note-Wooldridge-13)
* 2003 *Growl* – Rounder/Philo Records[[13]](#cite_note-Wooldridge-13)
* 2005 *Delirium Tremolos* – Rounder/Philo Records
* 2006 *Snake Farm* – Sustain Records[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* 2010 *A. Enlightenment B. Endarkenment (Hint: There is No C)* – Bordello Records ([Thirty Tigers](/wiki/Thirty_Tigers "Thirty Tigers")/[RED](/wiki/RED_Music "RED Music"))
* 2012 *[The Grifter's Hymnal](/wiki/The_Grifter%27s_Hymnal "The Grifter's Hymnal")* – Bordello Records (Thirty Tigers/RED)[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* 2015 *The Ruffian's Misfortune* – Bordello Records (Thirty Tigers/RED)
* 2017 *Tell the Devil That I'm Getting There As Fast As I Can* – Bordello Records (Thirty Tigers/RED)[[8]](#cite_note-Gage-8)[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* 2020 *Co-Starring* – [Big Machine Records](/wiki/Big_Machine_Records "Big Machine Records")
* 2022 *Co-Starring Too* – Big Machine Records, released March 18, 2022

## Books

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ray_Wylie_Hubbard&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Books")]

* *A Life... Well, Lived* (biography and memoir published in 2015)[[7]](#cite_note-Jurek-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Gage-8)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)
* *The Messenger: The Songwriting Legacy of Ray Wylie Hubbard* by author Brian T. Atkinson was published by [Texas A&M University](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_University "Texas A&M University") Press in 2019. The book includes forewords by [Jerry Jeff Walker](/wiki/Jerry_Jeff_Walker "Jerry Jeff Walker") and Hayes Carll.
